title: MacIntyre Storms Texas: Four-Shot Lead Heading to Masters
excerpt: Robert MacIntyre's brilliant second-round 64 at the Texas Open gives him a commanding four-shot lead with just one event left before The Masters next week.
body:
MacIntyre Takes Control in San Antonio

Robert MacIntyre is hitting form at precisely the right moment. The Scot fired a scintillating second-round 64 at the Valero Texas Open to storm into a commanding four-shot lead with 36 holes still to play. His 36-hole total of 130 represents the lowest scoring since the event moved to TPC San Antonio in 2010—a statement of intent with The Masters just seven days away.

MacIntyre's Lead
-4 Shots
36-Hole Score
130 (-14)
Course Record
Since 2010
Rounds Left
2

A Blistering Round

MacIntyre's Friday performance was clinical. He got off to a blistering start with an eagle on the 14th hole, then birdied four of his final five holes for a 64. When a player is striking the ball like this—precise, aggressive, and fearless—there's very little the field can do. Ludvig Aberg's second-place 67 left him seven shots back. Tommy Fleetwood, an ever-present threat at Augusta, slipped to ten down. The depth of MacIntyre's advantage is simply stunning.

Perfect Timing Before Augusta

There is simply no better position to be in heading into the week before golf's most prestigious major. MacIntyre has historically struggled with the pressure cooker of The Masters—he missed cuts in 2023 and 2024—but form like this suggests a mindset shift. A player in this kind of rhythm, hitting every fairway and making everything inside ten feet, carries genuine momentum into Augusta.

The Field's Challenge

For the chasers, this weekend at TPC San Antonio is about not losing further ground. Aberg will be looking to capitalise on any slip from MacIntyre, though a four-shot deficit entering round three is daunting. Fleetwood, who won here in 2022, knows the course and has the game to climb back. But make no mistake: this is MacIntyre's to lose. He's struck the ball beautifully, putted even better, and has the confidence only form can provide. The eyes of the golfing world will be watching how the Scot performs this weekend—not just for the Texas Open trophy, but for what it means for his Augusta chances.
